intro: Being able to estimate the value of powers and roots without a calculator is an important skill. It requires knowing some key values and using common sense to narrow down a result.
explanation:
Key squares and cubes to know:
Squares: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 121, 144, 169, 196, 225.
Cubes: 1, 8, 27, 64, 125, 216, 343, 512, 729, 1000.

Estimating square roots:
If the number lies between two perfect squares, the square root lies between their roots.
Example: Square root of 50. 49 = 7 squared and 64 = 8 squared, so square root of 50 is between 7 and 8, closer to 7.

Estimating cube roots:
Similarly, use known cubes.
Example: Cube root of 90. 64 = 4 cubed and 125 = 5 cubed, so cube root of 90 is between 4 and 5, closer to 5.

Negative and fractional indices:
x to the power -n = 1 / (x to the power n).
Example: 2 to the power -3 = 1/8.

x to the power (1/n) = nth root of x.
Example: 27 to the power (1/3) = cube root of 27 = 3.

x to the power (m/n) = (nth root of x) to the power m = nth root of (x to the power m).
Example: 8 to the power (2/3) = (cube root of 8) squared = 2 squared = 4.

Estimation strategy:
Round each value to 1 significant figure, then calculate.

worked_example.question: Evaluate without a calculator: (a) 125 to the power (2/3)   (b) 16 to the power (-3/4)
steps: Part (a): 125 to the power (2/3) = (cube root of 125) squared = 5 squared = 25.
Part (b): 16 to the power (-3/4) = 1 / (16 to the power 3/4) = 1 / ((fourth root of 16) cubed) = 1 / (2 cubed) = 1/8.

common_mistakes: Reversing the order of root and power in fractional indices — for x to the power (m/n), apply the nth root first, then raise to power m (or vice versa — both work but root first is simpler).
Forgetting the negative index means reciprocal — x to the power -n = 1 / x to the power n.
Guessing the square root of a non-perfect-square as the nearest integer — always identify the two perfect squares on either side first.
